                The Secretary of Labor has determined that
                the publication of this periodical is necessary
                in the transaction of the public business re-
                quired by law of this Department. Use of funds
                for printing this periodical has been approved
                by the Director of the Office of Management
                and Budget through February 28, 1981. Con-
                trolled circulation postage paid at Riverdale,
                Md.

                 Unless identified as copyrighted, articles and
                 tabulations in this publication are in the public
                 domain and may be reproduced without per-
                 mission of the Federal Government. Please

                 Labor Statistics.

                 Employment and Unemployment
                 Developments, September 1979




                    Employment rose in September and unemploy-         primarily among teenagers and adult women.
                 ment declined. The Nation's overall unemploy-         Teenage employment returned to the July level,
                 ment rate was 5.8 percent, down slightly from         following a dip of comparable magnitude in
                 6.0 percent in August but little different from the   August; employment of adult women rose in both
                 rates which have prevailed over the past year.        months.

                 BLS Establishment Estimates Revised
                 to March 1978 Benchmark Levels
                    Maxine Both




                     This issue of Employment and Earnings in-            were 1 percent or less except for mining, con-
                  troduces revised estimates for employment in            struction, and services. During the 1970's, the
                  nonagricultural establishments, reflecting the          employment estimates for the total nonagricul-
                  most recent and complete employment counts,             tural sector and for the major industry divisions
                  called benchmarks. The benchmark month for the          generally have varied from benchmarks by about
                  current revision is March 1978, and most employ-        0.1 percent. The magnitude of the current revision
                  ment series have been revised beginning with            exceeds the average for reasons to be discussed
                  April 1977. Hours, earnings, and labor turnover         later in this article. Table 2 indicates the magni-
                  estimates, which are weighted by employment,            tude of the differences arising from the current
                  are also subject to revision as a result of shifts in   and previous revisions, by year and industry
                  employment levels. Coincident with the bench-           division.
                  mark adjustment, all seasonally adjusted series            BLS monthly estimates of employment, hours,
                  have also been revised.                                 and earnings are published in considerable indus-
                     The benchmark review serves as a quality con-        try detail. Based upon the Standard Industrial
                  trol process by providing both a more accurate          Classification (SIC) system, estimates are pre-
                  measure of employment levels and a more ac-             pared at the industry level (four-digit SIC) for man-
                  curate perspective on trends. Normally, new             ufacturing and at the industry group level (three-
                  benchmarks are determined for March of each             digit SIC) for most nonmanufacturing industries.
                  year at the most detailed industrial classifica-        Within the 3-digit industry groups (manufacturing
                  tion for which estimates are made. The difference       and nonmanufacturing) for which employment
                  between the benchmark for March and the cor-            estimates are published, 32 of the 255 groups
                  responding estimate for March is assumed to             were revised by 5 percent or more. The larger in-
                  have accumulated at a constant rate over                dustries in terms of employment tended to have
                  the previous 12 months. Most series, therefore,         the smallest percentage revisions (table 3).
                  are adjusted by applying a ratio to the estimates          The March 1978 estimates and benchmark
                  to wedge out the difference over the period from        levels for the major industry groups (two-digit SIC)
                  the new benchmark to the preceding one, 12              in manufacturing appear in table 4. Of the 20
                  months earlier. The benchmark level is then pro-        major groups in this division, 13 were revised by 1
                  jected forward to the current month based on the        percent or less. Revisions were somewhat larger
                  trend shown in monthly reports submitted by a           for the basic three- and four-digit industries,
                  sample of employers. The estimates adjusted to          but almost three-fifths of these differed by less
                  the new levels are then aggregated through suc-         than 3 percent and only about one-sixth differed
                  cessively larger groupings to total nonagri-            by 5 percent or more.
                  cultural employment.

                ESTABLISHMENT DATA
                                                                                                    Seasonal Factors

                   The following tables present seasonal adjustment factors for all series in the establishment section of this periodical. These factors were derived using data through May 1979 The factors should be used
                with current estimates (adjusted to the March 1978 benchmarks) beginning July 1979.
                   The seasonal movements are measured in order to adjust the data statistically for such recurring events as warm and cold weather, crop-growing cycles, holidays, vacations, regular industry model change-
                over periods, and the like. These movements are generally the largest single component of month-to-month change in employment, hours, earnings, and labor turnover. The seasonal factors which follow
                enable the analyst to remove these influences from the data in order to determine more basic trends.

                                                                     Explanatory Notes

                                 These explanatory notes provide information on the concepts, methodology, and scope of Household Data
                                 (A tables), Establishment Data (B, C, and D tables), and State and Area Unemployment Data (E table) pub-
                                 lished in Employment and Earnings.



                                                                             Introduction

                   The statistics in this periodical are compiled from two major           Employment
                sources: (1) Household interviews, and (2) reports from employers.

                     Labor turnover is the gross movement of wage and salary work-        stoppage extends through the report period.
                 ers into and out of employed status with respect to individual
                 establishments. This movement, which relates to a calendar month,
                 is divided into two broad types: Accessions (new hires and rehires)      ESTIMATING METHODS
                 and separations (terminations of employment initiated by either
                 employer or employee). Each type of action is cumulated for a                The principal features of the procedure used to estimate em-
                 calendar month and expressed as a rate per 100 employees. The            ployment for the industry statistics are (1) the use of the "link
                 date relate to all employees, whether full- or part-time, permanent      relative" technique, which is a form of ratio estimation, (2) periodic
                 or temporary, including executive, office, sales, other salaried         adjustment of employment levels to new benchmarks, and (3) the
                 personnel, and production workers. Transfers to another establish-       use of size and regional stratification.
                 ment of the company are included, beginning with January 1959.
                     Accessions are the total number of permanent and temporary
                 additions to the employment roll, including both new and rehired
                 employees.                                                               The "link relative" technique
